speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sidney Finkel. I was born in--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that good enough?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Maybe-- now he can say something to the [? son. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. My name is Robert Segal. I was born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It is April 7, 1995. The survivor is Sidney Finkel. The interviewer is myself, Robert D. Segal. We're in Chicago, Illinois, in the United States of America, and the interview will be conducted in the English language. OK. My name is Robert D. Segal. Today's April 7, 1995. I'm here interviewing a survivor named Mr. Sidney Finkel. We're in Chicago, Illinois, in the United States of America. Could you please state your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sidney Finkel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: F-I-N-K-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Sidney?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-I-D-N-E-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth, Mr. Finkel?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was [? Shlomo ?] David Finkelstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell those names for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nope.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any other names you were known by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was known-- my Polish name was Sevek. S-E-V-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: December the 19th, 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: 63 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, could you-- what, what country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Piotrkow Trybulnski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-I-O-T-R-K-O-W. Trybulnski. T-R-Y-B-U-L-N-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like? You were born in 1931. What was your life like before the war? Could you describe that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a-- as a young boy, I was one of five children. I was the youngest one of five children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had-- the oldest one was my sister, Ronia, who was about, about 19 years older than I was. No, 27 years older than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was born about 1911 or 1912?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1912. Then there was my brother, Isacz. They called him Izzy. And he was born in 1914. And then there was my sister, Lola, who was, I think, born in probably 1912. And then there was a much younger sister. Her name was Frania. And she was probably born around 1922, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What about your parents? What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Lieb. L-I-E-B. Finkelstein. And my mother's name was Faiga. F-A-I-G-A. Gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately when was your father, Lieb, born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 1891 or so?

speaker: interviewee
text: Someplace like that. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What about your mother, Faiga?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was probably-- I would say she was probably maybe-- I don't-- I'm not sure, but she was younger than my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are any of your siblings still living today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have my sister, Lola, is still living in the United States in Arizona.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's in Tucson?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tucson, Arizona. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your brother, Isacz, lived until very recently. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. My brother died in 1992 in London, England. He died of prostate cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you had a brother and sister-- I'm sorry. You had a sister, Ronia, who, who died during the occupation. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My sister, Ronia, at the time of the war, was already married. She was married. She had her own apartment. And let me-- I'm going to describe her little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll describe them all each individually actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you want to go through your childhood first and tell us what it was like, that would be fine too. Whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I want to establish my family, and then I'll go into the childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- Ronia was the oldest one. She had blond hair and blue eyes. She did not look Jewish. She was a very modern woman for her time. She was a graduate of the Gymnasium, which was a-- like higher than the high school would be here, maybe second year of college. She played the piano. She was very modern, because this was the age where the Jewish community, especially my family, was coming out into more modern attire and modern way of, of thinking and doing things. They were really indistinguishable from the rest of the population at that time, which was not true going back 40, 50 years when we lived in little villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. What do you think made your family more modern?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the fact that I think all-- both my sisters and my brother were very active in the Zionist movement and the young Zionist organizations. They were the leaders there in our town. They were assisting others to go to, to Palestine at the time. As a matter of fact, I don't know if it's true or not, but I think my oldest sister, Ronia, did want to move to Palestine but my father objected to her leaving. He didn't think that the condition in, in Palestine would be suitable for her. He just-- I don't know if it's true or not, but that's the story I've heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so dealing with my sister, Ronia, she also was one of the very few Jews that worked for the utility- local electric utility company. And that was a-- according to my brother, that was a very prestigious job for a Jewish person to have, because Jews were not employed in government at the time. She was married, I think about 1936, to a very nice young man. I don't remember his name. Subsequently, I think he was one-- he was actually a policeman in the-- in the ghetto. And I think he assisted us a lot in-- in being-- in saving us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was his name Pion Kav?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That's not his name. I couldn't find his name. It's some place on the record.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I can't-- I couldn't find his name. I think maybe Bernstein or something like that, but I'm not sure. I've never seen any part of his family, any reference to his part of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned with Ronia belonging to, to some organizations, the Zionist organizations. Do you remember the names of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hassim something. Hassim Kovar. Some name like that. I think there were more-- yeah, I think so. Hassim something.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you-- you were saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she got married and she had her own apartment, a very nice apartment. And this was the sister that I remember taking care of me more than, than my mother, because she was-- I remember her as being very warm, very loving, very caring towards me. And I spent a lot of time in her apartment because I was, by far, the youngest one of the family. I was kind of a devil, really. I was very hard to control. Nobody could really control me. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were about five years old or so when Ronia was married, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. They didn't even invite me to her-- well, they did invite me to her wedding, but apparently, what happened was that they didn't want me at the wedding because they were afraid that I would make a commotion or something. That shows you how behaved I was. And they send me to my aunt, Rachel, who lived about-- oh, I don't know, maybe about a mile away or something like that. And she got so mad at the fact that I was not at my sister's wedding that I remember my, my cousin, my young cousin, took me on his bicycle and he wheeled me to-- to the-- The wedding took place in the house, in our apartment. We lived in a very big apartment. And there was this feast going on with the long table, table cloths, and lots of people sitting around there and stuffing their mouths. And I was so angry. I just pulled on the table cloths. It was just-- I said a few things in Polish that they were just-- wasn't too complimentary. So that was me in those-- in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your cousin who rode you in on his bicycle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember my cousin's name. But my aunt's was Rachel Ruth. Her last name was Rolnik. And she was my favorite aunt. She really understood me. My whole family didn't understand me. She Understood me. And my cousins were wonderful. I would be with them a lot. They would-- I would ride on their bicycles. And they had a farm. Like-- well, maybe it wasn't a farm, but it was like they had a big vegetable lot behind there. And they used to grow their own potatoes and things like that. And I used go there and harvest the potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: It just always amazed me that I-- how potatoes came out of the ground, being a young kid, you know? Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're just digging and all of a sudden, there are potatoes there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did-- was that in Piotrkow also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was like, on the outskirts of Piotrkov, if I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Streets?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember details of the feast, your sister's wedding feast, when you walked in? You said there was a big banquet table.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know there was a lot-- lot of food to eat and every body was stuffing their mouth. But that's all I remember. I don't remember anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your favorite foods back then when you were a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I really don't know. I think potatoes were one of them, but I don't have any favorites. I just-- there's so much memory that's blanked out that I just don't remember. I don't remember ever sitting down for dinner in my house or ever having, having Shabbat or-- just I remember a little bit about Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically that I was able to go into the closet and there was-- in the closet, there were all kinds of cookies and stuff and the traditional type of stuff clinging to paper. I remember finding them. I remember sponge cake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Macaroons?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember-- oh, I also remember-- right. And I also remember the, the peasants, when we used to deliver lots at Passover. They would deliver lots and lots of eggs to us, dozens and dozens of eggs. So there was a lot of baking going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Do you remember searching for the Afikomen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other memories of your boyhood do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Going to kindergarten.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Playing with a fire engine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a Jewish school, your kindergarten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember some, some- having some good friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I remember I was once invited to another kid's house, and this lady served cookies and tea or milk or something. And I thought was very unusual. I don't think we had that in the house. You know, that was something different.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were a boy, you mentioned you kept to yourself a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I would say-- I would say that I spent a lot of time by myself. Yeah. Just wandering around. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what did your father-- what was your father's business when you were a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was a little boy, my father's business in, in Piotrkow was a timber yard. He had-- the family had moved from a village outside of Piotrkov called-- what was it called? I got it on the-- Pzyglow. Pzyglow. It was called Pzyglow. Don't ask me to spell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is where they were from. At that time, around 1916, '18, when the Polish war of Independence was going on-- because the Russians had occupied Poland and the Pols were, you know, in revolt. And my father apparently did very well. You know, he helped in the-- I think in the armaments-- running armaments to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: All his three brothers did. Two brothers. He was one of three brothers. And they, they were quite wealthy. They had farms and horses and that's what-- and they had a lot servants and people. When my father used to walk outside, they used to-- other people, the peasants, used to take the caps off to him. He was like a lord. At least, that's the story. But then the depression came and he got wiped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was right before you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I was born in a very bad time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And He got kind of mostly wiped out. And that's when  they-- but I understand from my brother, they had a saw mill and a flour mill. It was just a flour mill by a river. And he sold that, and he was able to come to Piotrkow. I think they came like in 1922 to Piotrkov. And he had a timber yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right in the center of town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right next to the market, across the street from the market.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the market? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't. But it was a pretty good sized timber yard. There was lots of timber stacked. There was no hardware like it is in this country. It was just strictly timber. Lots and lots of timber. And we had a, a German dog, a German shepherd dog, that used to be a vicious dog. And my-- I was repeatedly warned to leave the dog alone. But of course, I didn't. They were all amazed that I was at the dog and, you know, I made friends with the dog. And he would be all over me. I'd love to just climb up on those big stacks of timber and I would just sit right on top-- they were high up-- and watch the sky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a pretty, pretty town you lived in? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I don't remember that much of the town. I only remember a few streets and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I spent more time-- in, in, in my summer time, my father had a-- had a flour mill and a saw mill I think in partnership with other people, which was like, I don't know, maybe 100 miles away or something like that. And we used to go on a train there and I used to spend my, my summers over there. And that was like I used to go up to the gate and the peasants used to come in with their corn and things to be ground into flour. And, and I used to take rides on them. But there was-- it wasn't all happy experience, because in the-- in the flour, no, on the timber-- no, not timber. Yeah. In the saw mill, it was, it was steam driven. And one day I was there because I was always adventuresome and looking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there and workmen were eating their lunch, sitting down. And they had these big wheels, like a steering wheel. And I was playing automobile. And I opened up the steam valve. And the saw came, came crashing down. You know? And I got so frightened, you know, that-- You felt lucky you weren't hurt, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or were you hurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wasn't hurt. No. But they-- there were really mean to me, these Polish workers. They would-- and also in the flour, because they would throw flour at me, sacks and things like that. They were not very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what-- do you think that had something to do with your religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. Yeah. They would call me Jew and things, even if I was the son--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Definitely. The son's owner, they didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were five, or six, or seven years old at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Yeah. And I was really scared of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm. Is-- are those the first memories you have of what you now know as anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be the very, very first memories, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Any other signs when you were a child of, of anti-Semitism besides that incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I was to-- I used to go to school, at that time, I was going to first grade, I guess, or second grade. Something like that. They would be throwing stones. There would be a bunch of kids. They would gather up on the corner and they would throw stones, and they would call me names. Dirty Jew, and things like that, in Polish, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I just couldn't understand why they were doing that, because I kind of admired them. I wanted to be part of the hooligans, you know? And-- They wouldn't let you become part of their gang?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No way. They would just beat me up. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ask your parents why, why they were doing it? Did they ever explain it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But they sent-- from then on, somebody was escorting me to the-- to school usually. I think the maid came with me. I used to cover my, my face up with my hands so they wouldn't see that I was Jewish. But I got very quickly, I was wondering why, and I got the idea that, that it was about being Jewish that made me bad, different, evil. And that's what-- that's why they were doing what they were doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you believe that you were bad or evil because they thought you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I couldn't understand it at the time, but I got-- I got the connection that being Jewish meant that you were being hated. That's very early in life, I got that message. That being Jewish was not something desirable to be, because that automatically puts you in danger. Other people would despise you and be violent against you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that, that, that was also-- you know, at that time, like, I got ice skates for my birthday. My father-- that was a very big deal, you know? And I-- I wanted-- I was just dying to feel what it felt like to have steel-- because from then on I was-- before that, I was just ice skating with my shoes and things like that. But to have ice skates. And I wanted to go to the park. And they said, nope. No way can you go to the park. You can't do it. You can't. So I skated on the streets with other kids. They just didn't want Jews in the park? Is that why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- so I understand they would beat you up. It was just any time that there were the two groups together, there would be violence. And I think this, this was happening in 1938 and '39, because Hitler had, had even-- anti-Semitism was there any way forever in Poland, but it was worse at these years because Hitler had stirred up this anti-Semitism, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the first time you, you heard the name of Adolf Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard it when-- I remember something-- I don't know if we had a radio or they went out and they bought one, this big Phillips radio. Phillips was a big name in Europe. And we listened to Hitler. Every time Hitler used make a speech, I would-- I would-- the family would be all gathered around the radio. And I would hear him, too. I would distinctly hear this German talk and the, you know, heil seig, heil, and Juden this and Juden that. I understood. I could-- I could sense it and I could feel the anger in his voice and the threats. And that made me very fearful, because I, I knew that my family was very fearful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you draw a connection between hearing what Hitler said and hearing what the boys at school, who were attacking you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very loosely. Very loosely. Not-- not really. This was something local that I could handle. But this was something I didn't understand at all. I didn't know. I had no-- at eight years of age, I had no conception of the world politics or anything like that, and neither was I interested in anything. I was just interested in myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. What other memories do you have of your early childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would go to-- also sometimes we would spend the summer in Pzyglow. It was the place where my parents were from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-- hang on. P-Z-Y-G-L-O-W. Something like that. And it was like a summer resort. There was a river there. And I remember that it was my younger sister, Frania, that would take me with them when there was-- I think there was a dance or something going on. And I would-- you know, she-- it was her job to take me with her wherever she went, and her girlfriend's. And I would go with her. And I remember in that town, in that village, there was somebody drowned that year. There was a big hole in the river, like, you know, it's a drop. And somebody drowned.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember bringing the body up and the people screaming and, you know, anguish and so forth. Other than that, childhood memories from Poland? School. I remember school. Did you do well in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, I did. School, I was just fascinated. Because I could-- I could-- when I learned to read and I would just walk the streets of Piotrkow and look at all the signs that before were-- didn't make any sense to me. And I could read all the signs above the store. I think that was really nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What street did you live on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something called-- I don't know. What is the name? Marzikov or something like that. I can't remember. It was 26 something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nartowiezo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nartowiezo. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: N-A-R-T-O-W-I-E-Z-O.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's on the map. It's-- I have a book that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. When you mentioned stores, did you remember any favorite stores of yours when you were a child in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember my father used to take me with his business associates to restaurants. And we used to bring around these Viennese pastries. They were terrific. That was a big, big deal for my father to take me. It wasn't like it is here where I could live every day, go to McDonald's, things like that. It was just a--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a real treat.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was absolutely a big treat. Also, I remember my brother's taking me to like, a hot dog place where the steaming hot dogs, Frankfurters. Yeah. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Sauerkraut?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Ham. No, well, ham, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did your family keep kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family did keep kosher. But once in a while-- I remember there was a store on the-- above-- below the apartment that we lived in. And I remember my mother would send me and my sister down there and we were able to get ham sandwiches, which tasted wonderful. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do you have any memory of going to shul? Going to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I used to go with my father to a synagogue. I can't tell you which one. But I-- those were one of the best moments that I had with my father was, was being in, in the synagogue. And it was a very warm feeling for me to be with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to play with the other kids outside. As a matter of fact, I remember one day there was a load of timber that came on Yom Kippur and he refused to take it in, you know? But he really wanted to, I think. [LAUGHTER] But he wouldn't do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- what-- what-- how religious was your family in terms of if you could put it into sects, like orthodox or what have you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody in Piotrkow was orthodox. There was no reform movement that I'm sure there wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were-- on the orthodox side-- we would have been probably the most enlightened. I think that's the word they used. Enlightened. We used to go to synagogue and everything like that, but-- well, you know, I just told you, my mother send us down-- she wouldn't tolerate having ham in the house, but outside of the house, I guess it was all right. And we were not fanatical religious. And also, I think the big distinction in my family was that the children did not learn the Hebrew in the traditional way, which at that time, was going to an old Jewish Heider, it was called. You know, where the-- there was usually one person that would teach these kids. And he would-- they-- you know, they-- and sometimes it could be quite brutal. The man whipped them and stuff like that. Not everyone, but, but this was the regular reputation. And they would learn-- they would learn the prayers and the holy books and things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- when I say-- a good example of my family being enlightened is the fact that my, my brothers and my sisters learned Hebrew as a language in the gymnasium. See? So they all spoke Hebrew. They were great Zionists. Wow. Did they-- did they-- did you know any Hebrew when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. See, the tragedy of it was that when my-- my turn never came to go to school, to the higher school. So I never learned. So I would have wished they weren't so enlightened. I would have learned more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any favorite songs of yours when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I do remember one song.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember? Would you like to sing it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something about [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Something-- something about two little kittens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that one of your favorite songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't want to sing it for the camera, do you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to love-- my maid used to read-- I think we read, twin? No? Sawyers? No, that's not the name of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sawyer. You know, about the Indians and Injuns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Injuns. Yeah. And-- oh, I loved those stories. In the forest and carrying guns. Yeah. We used to clip coupons off of coffee and things like that and send in for prizes and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any-- did they have movies in your town at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the movies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I Went to movies. I went-- I saw No, what's the-- they're playing it right now. The musical. What is the name of it?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw I went to the circus. The thing that stands out in my mind is a guy with no arms being able to fire a rifle with-- with his feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was in Piotrkow?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Everything was in Piotrkow. Mhm. It was a pretty big city, wasn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a town of roughly 60,000 people and about a third of the population was Jewish. I think a little bit under a third. Maybe like 18,000. And for the most part, it-- well, I know about it now. I didn't know it then. But it was-- it had a lot of cultural things. You know, we had all of the different clubs-- from chess club, to their own band. Lots of different famous rabbis, lots of learnings, their own school systems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lot of cultural thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And many different diverse organizations, you know, Jewish organizations-- from the Bund, who were socialists, to the ultra conservative, to-- There was lots. Jewish newspapers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were too young to really--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't know that from then. I just know it from now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.
